FT_IC1.evidence: Secondary analysis of ABCD Study data; n=10,907 participants from Year 2 annual assessment (ages 11-12) through Year 3 mid-year assessment

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures mobile phone ownership ('Do you have your own mobile phone?') and social media use ('Do you have at least one social media account?') as binary variables at age 11-12

FT_IC3.evidence: Longitudinal logistic regression using generalized estimating equations (GEE) method; separate models for each predictor-outcome combination with statistical tests reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports odds ratios and confidence intervals. Example: phone ownership with alcohol use: OR=1.34 [95% CI 1.18, 1.51], β=0.29 [0.16, 0.41], p<0.001; social media use with cannabis: OR=4.91 [2.84, 8.47], β=1.59 [1.04, 2.14]

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical secondary analysis of ABCD Study examining prospective associations between baseline phone/SM use and 18-month substance use outcomes using longitudinal logistic regression

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Addictive Behaviors, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: Phone ownership and social media use are independent variables predicting substance use outcomes (alcohol, nicotine/tobacco, cannabis). This is phone/SM→non-SM outcome, not phone/SM as dependent variable
notes: Paper 85 meets all inclusion criteria. E13 applies: smartphone ownership as exposure with non-SM outcome (substance use). E14: social media use predicts outcomes in cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data, 4.0 data release, Year 2 assessment; N=10,052 youth aged 11-12 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures 'screen time' and 'problematic media use' but neither meets criteria. Screen time is generic total screen time (not smartphone/SM-specific). Problematic media use is a composite scale (PMPU-SF) but does not specify smartphone or social media platform. Per FT-IC2: 'Generic total screen time or STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown' = NOT_MET

FT_IC3.evidence: Multilevel regression models predicting clinical concerns (CBCL, BPM, SI, NSSI); unstandardized and standardized coefficients reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports unstandardized β coefficients and standard errors. Example from Table 2: Problematic media use predicting CBCL total β=7.73*** [0.21], internalizing β=1.74*** [0.07], externalizing β=2.50*** [0.07]

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis using cross-sectional multilevel regression models examining associations between screen behaviors and clinical concerns

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Development and Psychopathology, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: Fails FT-IC2 (not a specific smartphone/SM variable). EC7 cannot apply when FT-IC2 is NOT_MET. Additionally, even if screen time/problematic media use were coded as MET, the paper includes cross-sectional analyses with non-SM outcomes (clinical concerns), which prevents EC7 from applying
notes: Paper excluded at FT-IC2. E2: Inside screen composite - whole-composite only without smartphone-specific breakdown = NOT_MET. E18: Generic screen time/media use without device clarification = NOT_MET

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data, release 5.1 (released July 2023); analyzed 4289 adolescents ages 11-14 years across 3 waves (2018-2022)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures gaming disorder and video game use but NOT smartphone-specific or social media. Paper assesses 'Video Game Addiction Questionnaire' (VGAQ) for gaming disorder and questions about video games on computer, console, phone unspecified. Per FT-IC2: 'Console or PC video games' and 'Video games unspecified platform → NOT_MET (ambiguous)'. Per E17: 'Video games unspecified platform → NOT_MET (FT-IC2)'

FT_IC3.evidence: Cross-lagged panel models (CLPMs) examining directional associations between psychopathology and gaming disorder; reports β coefficients and 95% CI with p-values

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports β coefficients with 95% confidence intervals. Example: psychopathology predicting gaming disorder (2-to-3 year): β=0.03 [95% CI, 0.002-0.06], P=.003; 3-to-4 year: β=0.07 [95% CI, 0.04-0.10], P<.001

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis using longitudinal cross-lagged panel models examining directional associations between baseline psychopathology and subsequent gaming disorder

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in JAMA Network Open, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: Fails FT-IC2 (gaming disorder, unspecified platform video games - not smartphone/SM-specific). EC7 cannot apply when FT-IC2 is NOT_MET. Additionally, psychopathology is the predictor and gaming is the outcome, which is the opposite direction of EC7 criterion (a)
notes: Paper excluded at FT-IC2. E17 explicitly applies: 'Video games unspecified platform → NOT_MET (FT-IC2)'. Paper does not meet smartphone or social media exposure criteria
